role_summary: 1968 Mexico City Olympics gold medalist in the 200 meters (world record 19.83); the raised-fist co-protester with John Carlos (catalog entry 62) on the October 16, 1968 medal stand; the moment now known as the 1968 Olympics Black Power salute.
ai_reading: Smith is named in the John Carlos (entry 62) catalog page as the gold medalist beside Carlos on the medal stand. The Russell-Carlos cosine (Russell entry 8 -> Carlos 719, Carlos -> Russell 271, the catalog's strongest athlete-activist signature) is documented on the John Carlos catalog page; Smith is the third figure of that organizing moment but is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page is added so the 1968 medal-stand triangle has its third party as a findable landing-place. The cultural placement of Smith and Carlos is symmetrical (co-protesters); the catalog's reason for surfacing Smith as a distinct page is the academic-citation discipline of not collapsing two named individuals into one entry. The corpus does carry Smith as a named subject: the about-snippets below are all from Carlos's own oral history (entry 62), where Smith recurs across the boycott origin, the medal-stand symbols, and the placement of the medal itself, the only interviewee in the audited corpus who discusses Smith at length.
biographical_paragraph: Smith was born June 6, 1944 in Clarksville, Texas, the seventh of twelve children of a sharecropper. The family moved to California's San Joaquin Valley when he was six; he attended Lemoore High School and then San Jose State, where he held world records in the 200 meters and the 400 meters by his junior year. At the October 16, 1968 Mexico City Olympics medal ceremony for the 200 meters (his winning time of 19.83 stood as the world record until Pietro Mennea broke it in 1979), Smith and the third-place finisher John Carlos each raised a black-gloved fist during the playing of the U.S. national anthem; Smith later explained the salute as a human-rights gesture rather than a Black Power gesture per se. Both were expelled from the Olympic Village by IOC president Avery Brundage. Smith later played briefly in the NFL with the Cincinnati Bengals and taught sociology and coached at Oberlin College and Santa Monica College. He published his memoir Silent Gesture in 2007.

interview_snippets.speaker: John Carlos
interview_snippets.lead_in: John Carlos, the bronze medalist who stood beside Smith on the 200-meter medal stand, traces the origin of the proposed 1968 Olympic boycott to a remark Smith made to the press in Japan:
interview_snippets.quote: Tommie Smith had made a statement in Japan about a possible boycott. And I think that came off the top of his head. It kind of snowballed to the point where people started talking about it.

interview_snippets.speaker: John Carlos
interview_snippets.lead_in: Carlos says he ran the 200-meter final intending to place second so that Smith would take the gold, and reads his own third-place finish, with the Australian Peter Norman taking silver, as providential:
interview_snippets.quote: To get on the victory stand, I didn't go there anticipating taking third. I was giving the medal to Tommie. But see, that's what God steps in.

interview_snippets.speaker: John Carlos
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing the symbols he and Smith carried onto the medal stand, Carlos names the black scarf Smith wore:
interview_snippets.quote: Tommie had a black scarf for all the sacrifice of black people in the land.

interview_snippets.speaker: John Carlos
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recounting his insistence that San Jose State include Peter Norman in the campus statue of the medal stand, Carlos draws a contrast between Smith and Norman:
interview_snippets.quote: I love Tommie Smith, but I don't think Tommie Smith would have ever did with Peter Norman, did in that sense.

sources.title: Tommie Smith with David Steele, Silent Gesture: The Autobiography of Tommie Smith
